section: Effect of temperature on the oxidation of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the oxidation study of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy at 600–900°C in dry air. It reports the alloy composition (Fe-7.54Al-0.65C with minor Mn, Si, P, S) and pre-oxidation processing (hot forged at 1200°C, homogenized at 1100°C/4h). The initial microstructure is two-phase ferrite + Fe3AlCx carbide (Figure 1). Oxidation tests used TGA for 24 h at 100°C intervals; parabolic rate constants (Kp1 and Kp2) were derived and listed in Table II. At 600°C, a parabolic-to-accelerated transition occurs with nodule formation and internal oxidation (Figures 3–5), and the oxide scale comprises outer Fe2O3, middle (Fe,Al)2O3, and inner Al2O3 (Table III). At 700–900°C, no internal oxidation is observed, and two parabolic rate regimes appear (Figure 2). Nodules decrease with increasing temperature and are absent at 900°C (Figure 7). A mechanism is proposed: at 600°C, CO/CO2-induced porosity enables inward oxygen diffusion, causing internal oxidation that blocks Al supply; at 700–900°C, higher Al mobility permits a healing Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 layer that suppresses internal oxidation. No decarburization is noted at any temperature. Key evidence includes TGA curves (Figure 2), oxide morphology (Figures 3–7), EPMA line scans (Figure 5), and quantitative point analyses (Table III). Subsequent chunks should connect these observations to further discussions on carbide stability, diffusion, and temperature-dependent oxidation transitions.
processing_route: hot forged at 1200°C from 7 cm to 2.5 cm
oxidation in dry air (100 ml/min) at 600, 700, 800, 900°C for 24 h
key_variables: parabolic rate constants Kp1 (<4h) and Kp2 (>10h–14h)
microstructure: oxide scale layers at 600°C: Fe2O3 / (Fe,Al)2O3 / internal Al2O3 (Figures 4,5, Table III)
healing Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 layer at 700–900°C
properties: nodule density and size vs. temperature
mechanisms: 600°C: nodule nucleation from Al2O3/FeO/CO, CO2, inward O diffusion through pores, internal Al2O3 precipitation blocking Al supply → non-parabolic kinetics
700–900°C: increased Al diffusion → healing Al2O3/FeAl2O4 layer → suppressed internal oxidation and nodule suppression at 900°C

Material systems: Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy; ferrite (α-Fe); Fe3AlCx carbide (K-phase); Fe2O3; (Fe,Al)2O3; Al2O3; FeAl2O4
Sample aliases: Fe-7.5Al-0.65C
Processing route: hot forged at 1200°C from 7 cm to 2.5 cm; homogenized at 1100°C for 4 h; surface polished to 1200 grit; oxidation in dry air (100 ml/min) at 600, 700, 800, 900°C for 24 h; heating/cooling rate: 100°C/min
Key variables: oxidation temperature (600, 700, 800, 900°C); parabolic rate constants Kp1 (<4h) and Kp2 (>10h–14h); presence/absence of nodules; internal oxidation depth; Al diffusion vs. temperature; carbide stability and decarburization
Microstructure: initial two-phase: ferrite + Fe3AlCx (Figure 1); nodules at 600°C and 700°C (Figures 3,4,6); oxide scale layers at 600°C: Fe2O3 / (Fe,Al)2O3 / internal Al2O3 (Figures 4,5, Table III); healing Al2O3 or FeAl2O4 layer at 700–900°C; no nodules at 900°C (Figure 7)
Properties: oxidation weight gain curves (Figure 2); parabolic rate constants (Table II); internal oxidation occurrence (only at 600°C); nodule density and size vs. temperature
Mechanisms: 600°C: nodule nucleation from Al2O3/FeO/CO, CO2, inward O diffusion through pores, internal Al2O3 precipitation blocking Al supply → non-parabolic kinetics; 700–900°C: increased Al diffusion → healing Al2O3/FeAl2O4 layer → suppressed internal oxidation and nodule suppression at 900°C
Figures or tables: Figure 1; Figure 2; Figure 3; Figure 4; Figure 5; Figure 6; Figure 7; Table I

context_update.summary: This chunk states a concluding interpretation: the observed difference in oxidation behaviour between 600°C and 700–900°C is caused by the temperature effect on aluminium diffusion. This reinforces the mechanism that higher temperatures enhance Al mobility, preventing internal oxidation and nodule formation at 700–900°C, while limited diffusion at 600°C leads to the breakdown of protective scaling.
mechanisms: temperature-dependent aluminium diffusion controls the transition in oxidation behaviour

This chunk states a concluding interpretation: the observed difference in oxidation behaviour between 600°C and 700–900°C is caused by the temperature effect on aluminium diffusion.
